feedback.text: == In addition to "Subscription with Dynamic Pricing" as a dark pattern, add dynamic pricing in general. Airplane tickets can wildly fluctuate in price depending on tracked interest - they start cheaper, but go up soon, whereas another person might still be able to see them on the lower price on a different PC/IP address. == Game-specific currencies obtained through real money are a problem, they obfuscate the cost through several layers - there might be more than one type of ingame currency with various exchange rates; there might be fake/real discounts at which these currencies are purchased for, like having a non-discounted price, but having many kinds of bundles that give several different value propositions so you never buy the non-discounted variant and where it's very hard to calculate how much you are buying something for for real, and claims on these bundles like "500% increased value!" and the like; and the bundles that give you the best value might give intentionally a little less currency than what you are likely to be using it for, forcing you to buy multiple times. == A centralized way to report fake/scam webshops would be nice. == There are lots of scam sellers in webmarketplaces too. For example, instead of it being a HDD/SSD with the advertised specs, in the case there is a micro SD card inside, or a hacked drive to indicate more storage space than it actually. Reporting these through the webshop give minimal consequence to the seller, letting them continue what they were doing. === It should be prohibited for publishers of any kind of digital media (movies, e-books, music tracks, video games, etc) to make copies they have already sold to customers inaccessible and/or unplayable in any way. Like Amazon taking away purchased e-books at any time, for any reason; Spotify and Apple doing the same with music youve bought (not rented); or a gaming distributor remotely disabling an already bought game or leaving it in an unplayable state when they stop their service or for any other reason, when it could still be technically playable; or a game publisher/developer leaving a game in an unplayable state after they stop supporting it. Closure of a service (like spotify or kindle completely disappearing) should not be grounds to also remove or prevent access to goods people already paid for, where it could be doable to leave people with access to these goods that they already bought. == Games in particular should be designed so that they can be left in some kind of playable state even after their support ends. The proposals for these are described in much more detail in the Stop Killing Games initiative (or the Stop Destroying Videogames EU initiative). Here is an unofficial dead games list as reference too: https://stopkillinggames.wiki.gg/wiki/Dead_game_list === Payment processors (like Visa, Mastercard, Paypal) should not be allowed to refuse payment processing based on what product is being bought if the product is lawful. For example, these payment processors are even right now acting as a global monopoly and pressuring gaming platforms to remove certain types of games and content (that the law would still allow), on the threat of not processing any payment for them at all, making these platforms unable to function. Donation platforms like patreon and many others are examples too, where these platforms would want to allow certain kinds of content, but due to payment processors they cannot. Payment processors are currently censoring the internet. === Anonymity should be possible on the internet. Providing a selfie, ID card photo or similar should only be allowed in cases where it is absolutely required, like creating a bank account. Platforms requiring personal identification for flimsy reasons like age verification should be outright prohibited, and trying to propose laws requiring unnecessary personal identification on the internet by EU member states should be discouraged or disallowed too.
